title: Managed Postgres via Low-Cost DBaaS
description: Use low-ops managed Postgres DBaaS providers (e.g., Supabase and managed cloud Postgres offerings) to reduce DB operations overhead; validate quotas, compliance, and scale limits.

cost.costNotes: Estimates are intentionally conservative; verify against current pricing before committing. Free tiers are quota-limited; production use may require paid plans and compliance agreements.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min $0 represents free tier usage (Supabase free: 500MB database, 2GB bandwidth; Render free: 90-day expiry, 1GB RAM). Max $250 represents small production usage across low-cost DBaaS providers: Supabase Pro ($25/month base + ~$50-75 for compute/storage overages), Render Starter Postgres ($7-20/month), or AWS RDS t4g.micro ($15-20) + managed services overhead. Mid-range production workloads (10GB storage, moderate traffic) typically cost $50-150/month. Enterprise-grade or high-traffic scenarios exceed this pattern's scope and would require standard managed database services ($500-5000+/month).
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption. Requires 1-3 days of engineering effort: evaluating provider quotas and compliance requirements (4-8 hours), migrating schema and data from existing database or designing new schema (8-16 hours), configuring connection pooling and backup strategies (2-4 hours), setting up monitoring and alerting (2-4 hours), and validating scale limits under load testing (4-6 hours). DBaaS platforms reduce operational complexity but require careful evaluation of vendor lock-in, compliance boundaries (SOC2, HIPAA, GDPR), quota limits, and egress costs. Team needs moderate expertise in database administration and cloud architecture. Estimated 20-30 engineering hours at $60-80/hour blended rate.
provenance.licenseCost: No upfront license costs. All mentioned providers (Supabase, Render, AWS RDS, Azure Database, GCP Cloud SQL) operate on pay-as-you-go SaaS models with no software licensing fees. PostgreSQL itself is open source (PostgreSQL License, BSD-style). Costs are usage-based only, billed monthly. Free tiers available for development/testing across most providers.

provides.reasoning: This pattern provides a managed PostgreSQL database, which is a relational database system. More specific than 'database' and standardized term.
Pattern specifically provides PostgreSQL as the database implementation, which is important for compatibility and feature-specific dependencies.
The core value proposition is that this is a managed service (DBaaS), reducing operational overhead - this is a key differentiator from self-hosted databases.
Legitimate capability representing operational simplicity through managed services, which is explicitly stated as a pattern goal and differentiates from self-managed alternatives.
Managed PostgreSQL services provide standard SQL query interfaces for database operations, which is a key capability for consuming applications.
Managed database services typically include automated backup and restore capabilities as part of their core offering, reducing operational burden.
PostgreSQL has native row-level security via CREATE POLICY and ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, enabling per-row access control based on session variables or JWT claims.
Managed Postgres DBaaS providers include automated backup management — configurable retention, point-in-time recovery, and backup scheduling — as a core managed-service feature.

Managed Postgres DBaaS providers provide backup recovery capabilities including point-in-time restore and snapshot-based recovery, reducing the operational burden of maintaining backup infrastructure.
Generic managed PostgreSQL supports XA distributed transactions via the PREPARE TRANSACTION mechanism (two-phase commit protocol).

requires.reasoning: DBaaS solutions require a cloud infrastructure provider to host the managed service (Supabase, AWS RDS, Azure Database, etc.).
Applications need network access to connect to the managed database service, typically over internet or VPC peering.
This is explicitly a cost-focused pattern ('cost-managed', 'lowcost' in name), requiring cost optimization as a driving constraint and selection criterion.

supports_nfr.reason: Low-cost managed Postgres DBaaS tiers typically cap at 99.9% availability SLA without high-availability tier upgrades.
Managed Postgres achieves at least 5ms p95 latency floor for typical SQL queries accounting for network overhead.
Managed Postgres p99 latency floor is typically 20ms accounting for network overhead and occasional query contention.
Automated failover in low-cost managed Postgres typically completes within 5-10 minutes for instance recovery.
Low-cost DBaaS tiers typically support up to 1000 queries/second (3.6M/hour) before requiring tier upgrades.
Low-cost managed Postgres tiers typically cap read throughput at around 10000 QPS before requiring read replicas or tier upgrades.
Low-cost managed Postgres tiers typically cap write throughput at around 5000 QPS due to single-writer architecture limitations.
Managed Postgres services include built-in audit logging capabilities that can be enabled or disabled based on requirements.
Managed Postgres supports all tenant isolation strategies from row-level security to dedicated databases per tenant.
Managed Postgres can store PII or non-PII data depending on application requirements and compliance posture.
Low-cost DBaaS providers offer GDPR-compliant data handling capabilities when deployed in appropriate regions.
Some low-cost managed Postgres providers offer HIPAA compliance options, though typically at higher tiers.
Managed Postgres services can support CCPA compliance requirements through data residency and access controls.
Low-cost DBaaS can support SOX compliance through audit logging and data integrity controls when configured appropriately.
Managed Postgres provides read-your-writes consistency by default and supports both strict and eventual consistency patterns.
Managed Postgres DBaaS provides strict durability through synchronous replication or supports relaxed durability for performance.

supports_constraints.reason: Low-cost DBaaS providers like Supabase, Neon, and cloud-native Postgres operate across multiple cloud platforms.
Managed Postgres DBaaS is a database infrastructure pattern accessible from any language with PostgreSQL client libraries.
Database infrastructure pattern that supports all platform types requiring relational data persistence.
Managed Postgres works with or without additional caching layers depending on application performance requirements.
Managed Postgres can store AI/ML model data and results whether or not AI inference is part of the application.
Managed Postgres supports both single-tenant and multi-tenant applications through various isolation strategies.
Managed Postgres can serve as the database for both batch processing and real-time transactional workloads.
Managed Postgres can participate in distributed transactions or operate as a standalone ACID-compliant database.
Managed Postgres supports real-time streaming through logical replication and can also serve non-streaming applications.
Some managed Postgres providers support pgvector extension for vector search, though not all low-cost tiers enable it.
Postgres supports JSONB for document storage alongside relational data, enabling hybrid document-relational patterns.
Postgres can serve simple key-value needs through single-column tables alongside relational data models.
While not optimized for graph queries, Postgres can handle limited graph workloads using recursive CTEs and extensions.
Some managed Postgres providers offer TimescaleDB extension for time-series, though not universally available in low-cost tiers.
Managed Postgres is optimized for OLTP workloads with ACID transactions and relational integrity guarantees.
Low-cost DBaaS Postgres is not optimized for heavy OLAP workloads; consider dedicated analytical databases instead.

provider.description: Low-cost managed Postgres provider. Supabase offers generous free tier with auth/storage. Neon provides serverless Postgres with scale-to-zero. Render/Railway offer simple deployment. DigitalOcean provides predictable pricing.

instance_size.description: Database instance size affecting CPU/RAM. Micro for prototypes (<1GB RAM), Small for small apps (1-2GB), Medium for production (4GB), Large for high-traffic (8GB+). Directly impacts cost and performance.

storage_gb.description: Allocated storage in GB. Start small and scale as needed. Most providers charge per GB. Consider data growth rate and backup storage requirements.

backup_retention_days.description: Number of days to retain automated backups. Longer retention increases storage costs but provides better disaster recovery options. 7 days typical for development, 14-30 for production.

connection_pooling.description: Enable connection pooling (PgBouncer) to handle more concurrent connections efficiently. Essential for serverless/lambda functions. Adds slight latency but prevents connection exhaustion.

high_availability.description: Enable multi-zone replication for automatic failover. Significantly increases cost (2-3x) but provides 99.95%+ uptime. Required for production systems with strict SLAs.

ssl_mode.description: PostgreSQL SSL connection mode. 'require' enforces encrypted connections without certificate verification; 'verify-full' adds hostname verification.

warn_nfr.reason: Read QPS approaching low-cost tier limits (10K QPS) will require read replicas or tier upgrades, increasing costs and reducing the value of the 'low-cost' positioning.
warn_nfr.message: db-managed-postgres: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (>5000). Approaching low-cost tier limits; may require read replicas or tier upgrades that increase costs significantly.
